help.overview: The Edit Satellite menu lets you change the orbital elements of the selected satellite (effectively rewriting its TLE) and save or load TLEs as files. It is the go-to tool for what-if experiments on a real object's orbit.
help.imgAlt: The Edit Satellite menu with orbital element fields
help.imgCaption: Each field maps to a TLE value. Period and Mean Motion stay in sync automatically.
help.fields:
• Epoch Year / Day: when the elements were measured: two-digit year and fractional day of year (e.g. 173.25 = noon on day 173).
  • Inclination: tilt of the orbit relative to the equator, in degrees.
  • Right Ascension: where the orbit crosses the equator northbound, in degrees.
  • Eccentricity: enter only the digits after "0." (e.g. 0001234 means 0.0001234).
  • Argument of Perigee: orientation of the orbit's low point, in degrees.
  • Mean Anomaly: where along the orbit the satellite was at epoch, in degrees.
  • Mean Motion / Period: revolutions per day and minutes per orbit; editing one recalculates the other.
help.howToUse:
1. Select a satellite, then open the menu from the bottom bar, press E, or right-click the satellite and choose Edit Sat.
  2. Edit the fields and click Update Satellite to apply. The satellite immediately moves to the new orbit.
  3. Update Epoch to Now re-times the existing orbit to the current moment, keeping the satellite over its present location.
  4. Save TLE exports the current elements as a JSON file; Load TLE applies a previously saved file.
help.tip1: Updates are rejected if the new orbit would dip below the atmosphere; a toast asks you to try different parameters.
help.tip2: Eccentricity is the most common typo: it is the fractional digits only, with no leading "0.".
help.tip3: Edits are session-only and never touch the real catalog. Reload the page to restore the original orbit.

errorMsgs.failedToPropagate: Failed to propagate satellite. Try different parameters or if you are confident they are correct report this issue.
errorMsgs.cannotCalculateDirection: Cannot calculate direction of satellite. Try again later.
